title: Greedy regex in _parse_findings causes wrong JSON extraction and potential backtracking
description: In orchestrator/agents.py _parse_findings(), the regex r'{[\s\S]*"findings"[\s\S]*}' uses greedy quantifiers that match from the FIRST '{' to the LAST '}' in the entire output. When agent output contains multiple JSON blocks or text before/after the JSON (common when agents prefix explanatory text), this greedily captures everything, producing invalid JSON that fails to parse. The fallback r'[\s\S]*' array match has the same issue. With large agent outputs (100K+ chars), the greedy \s\S patterns also risk slow matching. The function silently returns [] on parse failure, causing findings to be lost with only a warning log.
requirements: Replace greedy regex with a proper JSON extraction strategy (e.g. find balanced braces, or try json.loads on progressively larger substrings)
Use a non-greedy approach or bracket-counting parser to find the correct JSON block
Add a metric/counter for parse failures so they can be tracked in traces.db
Log the first 200 chars of unparseable output at WARNING level for debugging
done_criteria: _parse_findings correctly extracts JSON when output has text before and after the JSON block
_parse_findings handles outputs with multiple JSON-like blocks by finding the one with 'findings'
Parse failure rate is tracked in agent_metrics or events table
No catastrophic backtracking on 100K+ char outputs
context: The function processes output from claude -p subprocess calls which frequently include explanatory text. Agent outputs can be 100K+ chars. Regex r'{[\s\S]*"findings"[\s\S]*}' on line 456 will match from the first { in any prefixed text to the last } in the output, almost certainly producing invalid JSON for any non-trivial output.
